parentQuestion: Ordinarily, it is NOT possible to transplant tissues from one person to another, yet corneas can be transplanted without tissue rejection. This is because the cornea ________.

scaffolds.q: Tissue rejection occurs when the immune system attacks foreign tissue.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! T cells recognize foreign antigens and destroy transplanted tissue.

scaffolds.q: Immune cells travel through the bloodstream to reach tissues.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Blood vessels are the highways for immune cell surveillance.

scaffolds.q: The cornea is 'immune privileged' - it resists rejection better than most tissues.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Corneal transplants have ~90% success without systemic immunosuppression.

scaffolds.q: The cornea has NO blood vessels (avascular).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Blood vessels would scatter light. Cornea must be transparent.

scaffolds.q: Without blood vessels, immune cells can't easily reach the cornea.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! No vascular highway = no immune cell traffic = less rejection.

scaffolds.q: The cornea DOES have nerves (densely innervated - corneal injuries hurt!).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Nerves don't cause rejection. It's avascular, not anervous.

scaffolds.q: The cornea IS living tissue - it's not dead or non-functional.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Living cells that need nutrients (from aqueous humor, not blood vessels).

scaffolds.q: Corneal transplants succeed because the cornea has no blood supply.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Avascularity prevents immune cell access, conferring immune privilege.

scaffolds.q: The answer is 'has no blood supply' - avascularity enables transplant success.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not 'no nerves' (it has lots), not 'not living' (it is), not 'no connective tissue' (it has stroma). AVASCULARITY = immune privilege = transplant success.
